questions.text: Which of the following facilitates opening of stomatal aperture?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Decrease in turgidity of guard cells
options.explanation: Decreased turgidity in guard cells leads to stomatal closure, not opening.

options.text: Radial orientation of cellulose microfibrils in the cell wall of guard cells
options.explanation: This doesn't facilitate stomatal opening. It's not related to the mechanism of stomatal regulation.

options.text: Longitudinal orientation of cellulose microfibrils in the cell wall of guard cells
options.explanation: This facilitates stomatal opening because it allows the guard cells to elongate and create an opening when the microfibrils align longitudinally.

options.text: Contraction of outer wall of guard cells
options.explanation: Contraction of the outer wall helps facilitate stomatal opening by causing the guard cells to bow outwards, creating an opening for gas exchange.

questions.explanation: When turgidity increases within the two guard cells flanking each stomatal aperture or pore, the thin outer wall bulge out and force the inner walls into a crescent shape. This results in the opening of stomata. The opening of stomata is also aided by the radial orientation of cellulose microfibrils in the cell wall of guard cells rather than longitudinal orientation. Decrease in turgidity closes the stomata. Radial orientation of cellulose microfibrils in the cell wall of guard cells causes opening of stomata. Contraction causes decrease in turgidity hence closing of stomata. So option B is correct.

questions.text: Root pressure develops due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Root pressure is not a result of passive absorption; it's an active process.
Root pressure is associated with the active absorption of water and mineral nutrients by the roots.
Root pressure can develop when transpiration is low, not when it increases.
Root pressure can develop when there's high osmotic potential in the soil, driving water uptake into the roots.

questions.explanation: Root pressure is positive pressure that develops in the xylem sap of the root of some plants. It is a manifestation of active water absorption. So correct option is B.

questions.text: A column of water within xylem vessels of tall trees does not break under its weight because of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lignification provides structural support to xylem vessels but doesn't prevent the column of water from breaking under its weight.
Positive root pressure can push water up in the xylem, but it doesn't directly prevent the column of water from breaking under its weight.
Dissolved sugars help maintain water cohesion, but they are not the primary factor preventing the water column from breaking.

The tensile strength of water, due to its cohesive and adhesive properties, is the main reason the water column in xylem vessels does not break under its weight, allowing for efficient water transport in tall trees.

questions.explanation: Cohesion, adhesion and surface tension are the forces responsible for movement of water up the tracheary elements. Water molecules remain attached to one another by a strong mutual force of attraction called cohesion force. On account of cohesion force, the water column can bear a tension or pull of upto 100 atm. Therefore, the cohesion force is also called tensile strength. Its theoretical value is about 15,000 atm but the measured value inside the tracheary elements ranges between 45 atm to 207 atm. Water column does not further break its connection from the tracheary elements because of another force called adhesion force between their walls and water molecules. Another force called surface tension accounts for high capillarity through tracheids and vessels. So correct option is D.

questions.text: In a ring-girdled plant:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The shoot and root die together
options.explanation: This is not typically the case. Girdling disrupts nutrient flow from roots to the shoot, leading to the shoot's death before the root.

options.text: Neither root nor shoot will die
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Girdling usually results in the death of the shoot.
This is the correct outcome. Girdling disrupts nutrient transport, causing the shoot to die before the root.
This is not the typical result of girdling; the shoot usually dies first due to nutrient and water transport disruption.

questions.explanation: In girdling or ringing experiments, a ring of bark is cut from the stem. It also removes phloem. Nutrients collect above the ring, where the bark also swells up and may give rise to adventitious roots. Growth is also vigorous above the ring. The tissues below the ring not only show a stoppage of growth but also begin to shrivel. Roots can be starved and killed, if the ring is not healed after some time. The killing of roots shall kill the whole plant, clearly showing that bark or phloem is involved in the movement of organic solutes towards the root. Roots die first after that shoots die. Both will die slowly but first roots will die. Roots die first as tissues below the ring stop growing. So correct option is D.

questions.text: Transpiration and root pressure cause water to rise in plants by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the primary mechanism. Transpiration creates a "pull" on water, rather than a "push."
This option is partially correct. Transpiration "pulls" water, while root pressure "pushes" it to some extent.
Transpiration creates a negative pressure that "pulls" water upward from the roots. While root pressure can provide a minor "push," it is not the primary mechanism for water transport in plants.
This is a more accurate representation. Transpiration "pulls," and root pressure "pushes" water to help it rise in plants.

questions.explanation: The transpiration process, pulls water upwards with the help of cohesion and adhesion properties of water molecules. According to transpiration pull theory, due to transpiration, the water column inside the plant comes under tension. This is called ‘transpiration pull’. On account of this tension, the water column is pulled up passively from below to top of the plant (almost like a rope). Root pressure is the pressure that forces water, absorbed from the soil, to move through the roots and up (i.e., pushes it up) the stem of a plant. It may be due to both the osmosis of water from the soil into the root cells, and the active pumping of salts into xylem tissue which maintains a concentration gradient along which the water moves. Transpiration pulls upward while root pressure pushes water. First transpiration pulls water up and then root pressure develops pushing it into the roots and stem, hence first pulling then pushing. Transpiration process pulls water upward while root pressure pushes water into the roots and xylem. So correct option is D.

questions.text: Which one gives the most valid and recent explanation for stomatal opening?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Starch hydrolysis can provide energy for stomatal opening, but it is not the primary and most recent mechanism.
Photosynthesis in guard cells can contribute to stomatal opening, but it's not the primary mechanism.
Transpiration plays a role in regulating stomatal aperture, but it is not the explanation for the mechanism of how stomata open.
This is the primary mechanism for controlling stomatal aperture. The movement of potassium ions in and out of guard cells leads to changes in turgor pressure, causing the stomata to open or close.

questions.explanation: According to this theory, K + ion enter and accumulate in guard cells during daytime, causing opening of stomata and during night, K + ions move out of stomata and stomata closes. So option D is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following criteria does not pertain to facilitated transport?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Facilitated transport can reach saturation, meaning that when all transport proteins are in use, the rate of transport levels off.

Facilitated transport typically involves the movement of molecules or ions down their concentration gradient with the assistance of membrane proteins. It is not typically used for uphill or against the concentration gradient transport, which is characteristic of active transport processes.
Facilitated transport relies on specific membrane proteins, such as carriers or channels, to facilitate the movement of molecules.
Facilitated transport often displays high selectivity, where specific proteins allow only certain molecules or ions to pass through the membrane.

questions.explanation: Facilitated transport is the process of spontaneous passive transport or downhill transport of molecules or ions across a biological membrane via specific transmembrane integral proteins. The movement of molecules or ions is from a region of lower concentration to a region of higher concentration is uphill transport. Thus, the correct answer is option (B), 'Uphill transport'. This refers to when all proteins are used for transport in facilitated transport. Facilitative does require membrane proteins for transport. It means membrane is highly selective it allows only certain molecules to pass through while prevent rest of them.

questions.text: In land plants, the guard cells differ from other epidermal cells in having:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Most plant cells, including guard cells, have a cytoskeleton for structural support and transport.
Mitochondria are present in most plant cells, including guard cells, to produce energy through cellular respiration.
Endoplasmic reticulum is a common feature in plant cells, including guard cells, for protein synthesis and lipid metabolism.
Guard cells contain chloroplasts, which enable them to perform photosynthesis, a crucial function for regulating stomatal opening and closing by controlling turgor pressure.

questions.explanation: The leaf and stem epidermis is covered with pores called stomata (sing., stoma), part of a stoma complex consisting of a pore surrounded on each side by chloroplast­ containing guard cells, and two to four subsidiary cells that lack chloroplasts. The guard cells differ from the epidermal cells in the following aspects: The guard cells are bean­shaped in surface view, while the epidermal cells are irregular in shape. The guard cells contain chloroplasts, so they can manufacture food by photosynthesis (The epidermal cells do not contain chloroplasts). Guard cells are the only epidermal cells that can synthesis sugar. Both have cytoskeleton. Both have mitochondria. All cells (epidermal and guard cells) have endoplasmic reticullum. So correct option is D.

questions.text: Guttation is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is the movement of molecules from high to low concentration down the gradient.
Transpiration is the loss of water in vapour form from leaves.
Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from higher to lower concentration through semi-permeable membrane.

Various ions from the soil are actively transported into the vascular tissues of roots, water follows its potential gradient and increases the pressure inside the xylem. This positive pressure is called root pressure. The effect of root pressure is observable at night and early morning when evaporation is low and excess water collects in the form of droplets near the tip of leaves of many herbaceous plants. Such water loss in its liquid phase is known as guttation.

questions.explanation: Various ions from the soil are actively transported into the vascular tissues of roots, water follows its potential gradient and increase the pressure inside the xylem. This positive pressure is called root pressure. Effect of root pressure is observable at night and early morning when evaporation is low and excess water collects in the form of droplets near the tip of leaves of many herbaceous plants. Such water loss in its liquid phase is known as guttation. It is the movement of molecules from high to low concentration down the gradient. Transpiration is the loss of water in vapour form from leaves. Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from higher to lower concentration through semi permeable membrane. So correct option is D.

questions.text: Guard cells help in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Guard cells control the opening and closing of stomata, which regulate transpiration, the process of water loss in plants.
Guttation is primarily related to the release of liquid water from the leaf margins and is not directly controlled by guard cells.
While plant cells have defense mechanisms, this is not a specific function of guard cells.
Guard cells do not provide protection against grazing; this is a function often associated with thorns, spines, or chemical defenses in plants.

questions.explanation: Stomata are the main organs for transpiration. The stem and leaf epidermis are provided with numerous stomata. Diffusion of water vapour through the stomatal pores is known as stomatal transpiration. Transpiration occurs while the stomata are open for the passage of carbon dioxide and oxygen during photosynthesis. Stomatal opening and closing is regulated by the movement of guard cells. It is loss of water in droplets form from leaves, but this process occurs under certain conditions. It is not a regular process. Guard cells are photosynthetic cells and help in transpiration. They donot protect against grazing. So correct option is A.

questions.text: The rupture and fractionation do not usually occur in the water column in vessels/tracheids during the ascent of sap because of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While the gravitational pull does exist, it is not the primary factor preventing rupture and fractionation in the water column of plant vessels and tracheids. Plants have mechanisms to counteract the effects of gravity.

This is a significant factor in the ascent of sap. Transpiration creates a negative pressure in the leaves, causing water to be pulled up through the plant. However, transpiration alone cannot explain the prevention of rupture and fractionation.

Lignified thick walls provide structural support to plant vessels and tracheids, but they are not the main reason for the prevention of rupture and fractionation. Thick walls help with stability but are not directly related to the phenomenon.

This is the correct answer. Cohesion refers to the tendency of water molecules to stick together, and adhesion refers to the attraction of water molecules to the walls of the vessels. These properties of water create a continuous, unbroken water column in the plant's vascular system, allowing water to be pulled up without rupture or fractionation. Cohesion and adhesion play a crucial role in the movement of water through plants.

questions.explanation: The transpiration process, pulls water upwards with the help of cohesion and adhesion properties of water molecules. According to transpiration pull theory, due to transpiration, the water column inside the plant comes under tension. This is called ‘transpiration pull’. On account of this tension, the water column is pulled up passively from below to top of the plant (almost like a rope). Root pressure is the pressure that forces water, absorbed from the soil, to move through the roots and up (i.e., pushes it up) the stem of a plant. It may be due to both the osmosis of water from the soil into the root cells, and the active pumping of salts into xylem tissue which maintains a concent. So correct option is D.

questions.text: Two cells A and B are contiguous. Cell A has an osmotic pressure of 10 atm, turgor pressure of 7 atm, and diffusion pressure deficit of 3 atm. Cell B has an osmotic pressure of 8 atm, turgor pressure of 3 atm, and diffusion pressure deficit of 5 atm. The result will be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This would occur if the water potential of both cells A and B were equal. However, in this case, there are differences in their water potentials, so this option is not correct.
This would occur if the water potential of both cells A and B were equal. However, based on the provided values, their water potentials are not equal, so this option is not correct.

options.text: Movement of water from cell A to B
options.explanation: Water moves from a region of higher water potential (higher turgor pressure and lower diffusion pressure deficit) to a region of lower water potential. In this case, the water potential is higher in cell A, so water will move into cell B.

options.text: Movement of water from cell B to A
options.explanation: This would happen if cell B had a higher water potential than cell A. However, based on the provided values, cell A has a higher water potential, so water will move from A to B, not the other way around.

questions.explanation: Diffusion pressure deficit is the reduction in the diffusion pressure of water in a system over its pure state. It is given by DPD = O.P – W.P (T.P). DPD determines the direction of net movement of water. It is always from an area or cell of lower DPD to the area or cell of higher DPD. So, cell A having lower DPD, water will move from cell A to B. So correct option is C.

questions.text: The translocation of organic solutes in sieve-tube members is supported by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cytoplasmic streaming is the movement of cytoplasm within a cell but is not the primary mechanism behind the long-distance transport of organic solutes in the phloem.
Root pressure and transpiration pull are involved in the movement of water (xylem transport) but are not directly responsible for the transport of organic solutes in the phloem.

P-proteins, also known as phloem proteins, are involved in sealing off damaged sieve-tube members to prevent the loss of sap. While they play a role in phloem physiology, they are not the primary drivers of organic solute translocation.

options.text: Mass flow involving a carrier and ATP
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The movement of organic solutes in the phloem is driven by mass flow. Specialized cells called sieve-tube members transport organic solutes, such as sugars, through the phloem by utilizing carrier proteins and energy in the form of ATP. This mass flow mechanism allows for the efficient and long-distance transport of nutrients throughout the plant.

questions.explanation: According to the mass flow hypothesis, the transport of organic solutes takes place from source to sink, this transport also depends on metabolic energy. According to the cytoplasmic streaming hypothesis (which was given by de Vries, in 1885) the transport of organic solutes takes place by a combination of diffusion and cytoplasmic streaming. Cytoplasmic streaming carries organic solutes from one end to the other end of the sieve tube. P-proteins have a role as a defense against phloem-feeding insects and the sealing of damaged sieve tubes. So, the correct answer is 'Mass flow involving a carrier and ATP'. So correct option is C.

questions.text: Potometer works on the principle of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Osmotic pressure is related to the movement of water into plant cells through osmosis, but it is not the primary principle behind the operation of a potometer. A potometer measures the rate of water loss from the plant due to transpiration.

options.text: Amount of water absorbed equals the amount transpired
options.explanation: This is the correct principle. A potometer is designed to measure the rate at which water is taken up by a plant (absorbed by the roots) and compare it to the rate of water loss through transpiration (water vapor released through the plant's leaves). The device is set up in a way that any water taken up by the plant corresponds to the water lost through transpiration, allowing for the measurement of the transpiration rate.

Root pressure is a force that can contribute to the movement of water in the plant, particularly in the xylem. However, it is not the primary principle utilized in a potometer. A potometer primarily focuses on measuring transpiration rates.

options.text: Potential difference between the tip of the tube and that of the plant
options.explanation: This is not the primary principle of a potometer. The potometer measures the rate of water uptake and loss, but it doesn't directly measure potential differences between the tip of the tube and the plant.

questions.explanation: Potometer is an instrument or apparatus with the help of which, rate of transpiration can be measured. Main types of potometers are as under: Simple potometer, Farmer’s potometer and Ganong’s potometer. The whole instrument is made of glass and is consisted of a long tube, having a side tube, bent at right angles. A fresh plant shoot is cut underwater and is inserted into the side tube through a cork, fitted into the mouth of this tube. The whole apparatus is filled with water and the joints are made air tight. The apparatus is placed in the sunlight. Air bubble enters the tube and after this lower end of the tube is placed in the beaker, containing water. Water is absorbed by the shoot and is transpired through the leaves. Transpiration pull is created and the air bubble begins to move along with the transpiration pull. Readings are taken for the air bubble and thus amount of water absorbed and transpired is calculated. So option B is correct.

questions.text: Stomata of a plant open due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Stomatal opening is largely regulated by the movement of potassium ions into the guard cells that surround the stomatal pore. When potassium ions enter the guard cells, water follows by osmosis, causing the cells to swell and the stomata to open.
The efflux (movement out) of potassium ions from guard cells would not lead to stomatal opening. Instead, it would cause the guard cells to lose water and shrink, leading to stomatal closure.

The influx of hydrogen ions is not the primary factor responsible for stomatal opening. While pH changes within the guard cells can play a role in stomatal regulation, it is not the primary mechanism for stomatal opening.

Calcium ions can be involved in stomatal regulation, but they are not the primary ions responsible for stomatal opening. Calcium ions may play a role in the signal transduction pathway that regulates stomatal movement.

questions.explanation: The mechanism of opening and closing of stomata is explained by active K + ion transport theory. So option A is correct.

questions.text: Main function of lenticel is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lenticels are not primarily involved in transpiration. Transpiration refers to the loss of water vapor from the aerial parts of the plant, primarily through stomata on leaves.
Guttation is the process of exuding liquid water, typically from the margins of leaves, and it usually occurs in the early morning or at night. Lenticels are not directly responsible for guttation.

This is the correct answer. Lenticels are small openings in the bark of woody stems that allow for the exchange of gases (such as oxygen and carbon dioxide) between the internal tissues of the plant and the external atmosphere. Gaseous exchange is crucial for cellular respiration and metabolic processes in the plant.

Bleeding in plants refers to the exudation of sap or liquid from wounds or cuts in the plant, and it can happen through lenticels in some cases. However, the primary function of lenticels is gaseous exchange, not bleeding.

questions.explanation: Lenticels generally appear under stomata. The lenticel of phellogen itself also has intercellular spaces. Because of this relatively open arangement of cells, the lenticels are regarded as structures permitting the entry of air through the periderm. Lenticels are characterstics of woody stem but they are also found in roots of trees and other perennials for entry of oxygen through them. So option C is correct.

questions.text: Opening and closing of stomata is due to the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While hormones can play a role in the regulation of stomatal opening and closing, the immediate mechanism responsible for the physical changes in guard cells is the change in their turgor pressure.

options.text: Change in turgor pressure of guard cells
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The opening and closing of stomata are primarily regulated by changes in the turgor pressure of the specialized cells called guard cells that surround the stomatal pore. When these guard cells gain water and increase in turgor pressure, they swell and cause the stomatal pore to open. Conversely, when they lose water and decrease turgor pressure, they shrink, leading to stomatal closure.

Gaseous exchange refers to the exchange of gases like oxygen and carbon dioxide, which occurs through stomata. However, gaseous exchange itself does not directly control the opening and closing of stomata.

Respiration is a metabolic process in plants, but it is not the direct mechanism responsible for the opening and closing of stomata. The control of stomatal aperture is primarily related to the turgor pressure of guard cells.

questions.explanation: It is turgor pressure in guard cells which determines the opening and closing of stomata. When the guard cells are swelled because of water absorption, it leads to opening of the stomatal pores. Once the water is lost, the guard cells become flaccid and the stomata closes. Also, during the daytime stomata generally remain open and during the night they remain closed. So correct option is B.

questions.text: Passive absorption of minerals depends on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This can be the correct answer because passive absorption of minerals is influenced by temperature, but it is also affected by metabolic inhibitors. Metabolic inhibitors can block or slow down active transport processes, allowing passive transport mechanisms like diffusion to play a more significant role in mineral uptake by plant roots.

This is the correct answer. Passive absorption of minerals is influenced by temperature, but it is also affected by metabolic inhibitors. Metabolic inhibitors can block or slow down active transport processes, allowing passive transport mechanisms like diffusion to play a more significant role in mineral uptake by plant roots.

Metabolic inhibitors can indeed affect the absorption of minerals by interfering with active transport processes, but they are not the sole factor involved in passive absorption. Temperature also plays a role.
Humidity levels primarily influence transpiration and water uptake by plants but are not directly related to the passive absorption of minerals.

questions.explanation: Rate of salt absorption increases when temperature increases but to a certain limit as salt absorption is inhibited at higher temperature because certain enzymes are not active at higher temperature and carriers are not synthesised. These carriers are required for active transport of salts from outer space in inner space. So correct option is A.

questions.text: Glycolate induces opening of stomata in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Oxygen is usually present in the atmosphere and is not a specific trigger for stomatal opening.
Glycolate is involved in the photorespiratory pathway, and it can lead to stomatal opening in response to low CO2 concentration, which is a condition associated with the photorespiratory process.
Glycolate does not induce the opening of stomata in high carbon dioxide concentrations rather it induces the opening of stomata in low carbon dioxide concentrations.
Complete absence of CO2 is not a typical physiological condition for plants, and it would likely lead to stomatal closure rather than opening.

questions.explanation: Zelitch (1963) suggested that glycolic acid is formed in the guard cells. This acid is formed under a low concentration of CO. Glycolate formed gives rise to carbohydrates. Under this condition, the osmotically active material is produced and ATP synthesis also takes place. ATP is produced during the glyoxylate glycolate shuttle. This ATP helps in the active pumping of water in the guard cells and stomata open. Stomata close when this process is reversed. So option B is correct.

questions.text: Loading of phloem is related to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Loading of phloem refers to the process by which sugars, primarily sucrose, are actively transported from source tissues (where they are produced or stored) into the phloem. This loading process involves increasing the sugar content in the phloem to create a concentration gradient that drives the movement of sugars from source to sink tissues.
The elongation of phloem cells is related to their growth and structural support but is not directly connected to the loading of sugars into the phloem.

The separation of phloem parenchyma cells is not typically associated with the loading of sugars. Phloem parenchyma cells are involved in various functions, including storage and support, but they do not directly load sugars into the phloem.
Phloem fibers provide structural support to the phloem tissue but are not directly involved in the loading of sugars into the phloem.

questions.explanation: When the phloem cells, just near the source, for example, green leaves attain a higher concentration of sugars, it is called the process of phloem loading. Sucrose is photosynthesized in the chloroplasts of mesophyll cells of leaves. Mesophyll cells are connected with each other through plasmodesmata. Similarly, plasmodesmata are also present between the mesophyll cells and companion cells and also between mesophyll cells and sieve tubes. Their plasmodesmata are the “channels” meant for the passage of sucrose. So correct answer is A.

questions.text: The movement of ions against the concentration gradient will be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct option. Active transport involves the movement of materials across the membrane against the concentration gradient of the solute particles. It requires energy in the form of ATP and carrier molecules

Osmosis is the movement of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane from an area of lower solute concentration to an area of higher solute concentration. It is a specific type of passive transport and does not involve the movement of ions against the concentration gradient.

Diffusion is the passive movement of molecules or ions from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. It is a type of passive transport and does not involve the movement of ions against the concentration gradient.

This option is incorrect because osmosis and diffusion are forms of passive transport and do not involve the movement of ions against the concentration gradient. Only active transport requires energy to move ions against the concentration gradient.

questions.explanation: Active transport involves the movement of materials across the membrane against the concentration gradient of the solute particles. It requires energy in the form of ATP and carrier molecules

questions.text: In soil, water available for plants is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the water that drains rapidly through the soil due to the force of gravity and is not available for plants as it moves beyond the root zone.
This refers to water molecules that are tightly bound to soil particles and are not available for plant uptake.

Capillary water refers to the water in the soil that is held between soil particles by capillary forces and is available for plant roots to absorb. It is held in the soil's smaller pores and is easily accessible to plants.
This is the water that is absorbed as a thin film around soil particles and is unavailable to plants due to its strong adhesion to the soil surfaces.

questions.explanation: Capillary water refers to the water in soil that is held between soil particles by capillary forces and is available for plant roots to absorb. It is held in the soil's smaller pores and is easily accessible to plants. So correct option is C.

questions.text: The water potential and osmotic potential of pure water are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Water potential cannot be higher than 0 for any solution, as pure water is the reference point. Hence, the water potential cannot be 100 or 200.

The water potential of pure water is always 0, as it is the reference point for comparison. The osmotic potential of pure water is 100 because pure water has the maximum ability to do work by moving into a solution against its concentration gradient
The osmotic potential of pure water is not zero, as explained earlier. It is 100, representing its maximum ability to draw water in.
While the water potential of pure water is indeed zero, the osmotic potential cannot be zero. It is 100, representing its maximum ability to draw water in.

questions.explanation: Water potential of pure water is always 0, as it is the reference point for comparison. Osmotic potential of pure water is 100, because pure water has the maximum ability to do work by moving into a solution against its concentration gradient. So correct option is B.

questions.text: When a cell is fully turgid, which of the following will be zero?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Turgor pressure is the pressure exerted by the water inside the cell against the cell wall, and it is not zero when the cell is turgid.
Water potential becomes zero when the cell is fully turgid, indicating equilibrium between the water inside and outside the cell.
Wall pressure is the pressure exerted by the cell wall against the water inside the cell, and it is also not zero in a turgid cell.
Osmotic pressure is the pressure exerted by the solutes in the cell's cytoplasm and is also not zero in a turgid cell.

questions.explanation: Water potential becomes zero when the cell is fully turgid, indicating equilibrium between the water inside and outside the cell. So correct option is B.

questions.text: With an increase in the turgidity of a cell, the wall pressure will be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because turgor pressure does not fluctuate, it increases with an increase in water content in the central vacuole.
This option is incorrect because an increase in turgidity leads to an increase in turgor pressure and subsequently an increase in wall pressure. It does not remain unchanged.

When the turgidity of a cell increases, it means that the cell's central vacuole is filled with more water, exerting an increased pressure on the cell wall. This increased pressure is called turgor pressure. As a result, the wall pressure of the cell will also increase.
This option is incorrect because an increase in turgidity leads to an increase in wall pressure, not a decrease.

questions.explanation: When the turgidity of a cell increases, it means that the cell's central vacuole is filled with more water, exerting an increased pressure on the cell wall. This increased pressure is called turgor pressure. As a result, the wall pressure of the cell will also increase.So correct option is C.

questions.text: Bidirectional translocation of solutes takes place in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parenchyma cells are the most common type of plant cells and are involved in various functions such as storage, photosynthesis, and secretion. While they can facilitate some local movement of solutes within tissues, they do not perform long-distance bidirectional translocation like the phloem.

The cambium is a thin layer of meristematic tissue in the stems and roots of woody plants. Its primary function is to produce new xylem and phloem cells for secondary growth, allowing the plant to increase in girth. The cambium does not play a direct role in the translocation of solutes.

The xylem is responsible for the upward transport of water and mineral nutrients from the roots to the rest of the plant. It is a unidirectional transport system and is not involved in the bidirectional translocation of solutes like the phloem.

Bidirectional translocation of solutes takes place in the phloem. The phloem is responsible for transporting organic compounds, such as sugars and amino acids, throughout the plant in both upward (from the leaves to other parts) and downward (from the source to the sink) directions. This bidirectional movement of solutes is essential for the distribution of nutrients and energy to different parts of the plant.

questions.explanation: Bidirectional translocation of solutes takes place in the phloem. The phloem is responsible for transporting organic compounds, such as sugars and amino acids, throughout the plant in both upward (from the leaves to other parts) and downward (from the source to the sink) directions. This bidirectional movement of solutes is essential for the distribution of nutrients and energy to different parts of the plant. So option D is correct.

questions.text: When water enters in roots due to diffusion, is termed as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water across a semipermeable membrane from an area of lower solute concentration to an area of higher solute concentration. While osmosis plays a role in passive absorption, it specifically refers to the movement of water through a semipermeable membrane.

When water enters roots due to diffusion, it is termed passive absorption. Passive absorption is the process by which water and mineral nutrients enter the roots of plants passively, driven by the concentration gradient between the soil and the root cells. It does not require energy expenditure by the plant.

Endocytosis is a process in which cells engulf external materials by forming a vesicle around them. It is mainly observed in animal cells for the uptake of large molecules or particles. Endocytosis is not involved in the absorption of water in plant roots.

Active absorption is the process of absorbing water and nutrients by the roots of plants using energy from adenosine triphosphate (ATP) through the activity of carrier proteins in the cell membranes. This process requires energy expenditure by the plant and is distinct from passive absorption, which does not involve energy consumption.

questions.explanation: When water enters roots due to diffusion, it is termed as passive absorption. Passive absorption is the process by which water and mineral nutrients enter the roots of plants passively, driven by the concentration gradient between the soil and the root cells. It does not require energy expenditure by the plant. So correct option is B.

questions.text: The movement of water, from one cell of the cortex to an adjacent one in the roots, is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Accumulation of inorganic salts in the cells
options.explanation: Inorganic salts may affect osmotic pressure, but they do not directly cause the movement of water between cells. Water movement is primarily driven by water potential gradients.

options.text: Accumulation of organic compounds in the cells
options.explanation: Organic compounds may also influence osmotic pressure, but they are not the main factor responsible for water movement between cells. Water potential gradient is the key driver of water flow.

Water moves from one cell of the cortex to an adjacent one in the roots due to the difference in water potential between the cells. Water potential is the potential energy of water to move from one place to another, and it is influenced by factors such as solute concentration and pressure. When there is a difference in water potential between two adjacent cells, water will move from the region of higher water potential to the region of lower water potential until equilibrium is reached.

While water potential includes chemical potential, this option is too general and does not specifically address the driving force behind water movement between cells. The more specific answer is the water potential gradient.

questions.explanation: Water moves from one cell of the cortex to an adjacent one in the roots due to the difference in water potential between the cells. Water potential is the potential energy of water to move from one place to another, and it is influenced by factors such as solute concentration and pressure. When there is a difference in water potential between two adjacent cells, water will move from the region of higher water potential to the region of lower water potential until equilibrium is reached. So correct option is C.

questions.text: Translocation of carbohydrate nutrients usually occurs in the form of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glucose is a monosaccharide, and while it is an essential carbohydrate nutrient, it is not typically the form in which carbohydrates are translocated within plants.
Maltose is a disaccharide composed of two glucose molecules. While it may be involved in internal plant processes, it is not the primary form of carbohydrate translocation in plants.
Starch is a polysaccharide and is the storage form of carbohydrates in plants. It is not directly translocated through the phloem; instead, it is broken down into sucrose for transport.

Sucrose is the main form of translocated carbohydrate nutrient in plants. It is a disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose and is transported through the phloem from source (sites of production, e.g., leaves) to sink (sites of utilization, e.g., roots, fruits) tissues in plants.

questions.explanation: Sucrose is the main form of translocated carbohydrate nutrient in plants. It is a disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose and is transported through the phloem from source (sites of production, e.g., leaves) to sink (sites of utilization, e.g., roots, fruits) tissues in plants. So the correct option is D.

questions.text: In guard cells when sugar is converted into starch, the stomatal pore:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When sugar is converted into starch in guard cells, the stomatal pore closes completely. This process occurs during the night when photosynthesis cannot take place, and the conversion of sugar to starch leads to a decrease in the osmotic potential in the guard cells. As a result, water moves out of the guard cells, causing them to lose turgidity and close the stomatal pore.
This option is incorrect because the conversion of sugar into starch leads to a decrease in turgidity, causing the stomatal pore to close, not open partially.
This option is incorrect because the conversion of sugar into starch causes the stomatal pore to close completely, not open fully.
This option is incorrect because the conversion of sugar into starch results in changes in turgidity, leading to the closure of the stomatal pore, not remaining unchanged.

questions.explanation: When sugar is converted into starch in guard cells, the stomatal pore closes completely. This process occurs during the night when photosynthesis cannot take place, and the conversion of sugar to starch leads to a decrease in the osmotic potential in the guard cells. As a result, water moves out of the guard cells, causing them to lose turgidity and close the stomatal pore. So correct option is A.

questions.text: At constant temperature, the rate of transpiration will be higher at:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Transpiration rates are typically lower at sea level compared to higher altitudes.
This is not a natural condition for plant growth, and the rate of transpiration would be low due to limited oxygen availability.
Transpiration rates are generally higher at higher altitudes because the lower air pressure at higher altitudes can lead to increased transpiration to maintain water balance in plants.
At constant temperature, the rate of transpiration will be higher at 1.5 km above the sea level. At lower atmospheric pressure there is increase in the rate of evaporation. So correct option is D.

questions.explanation: At constant temperature, the rate of transpiration will be higher at 1.5 km above the sea level. At lower atmospheric pressure there is increase in the rate of evaporation. So correct option is D.

questions.text: Conversion of starch to organic acids is required for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stomatal opening is primarily regulated by other mechanisms, such as the uptake and loss of ions and water by guard cells.
The conversion of starch to organic acids occurs during the nighttime, and these organic acids help drive the closing of stomata by promoting a decrease in turgor pressure in guard cells.
Stomatal formation involves different processes and is not directly related to the conversion of starch to organic acids.
Stomatal activity encompasses both opening and closing, and the conversion of starch to organic acids is specifically involved in the closing of stomata.

questions.explanation: There is evidence to believe that besides organic acids the turgidity of guard cells is usually controlled by K + , Cl – and H + . The opening of stomata is initiated by exertion of H + by guard cells, intake if K + and Cl disappearance of starch and appearance of organic acids like malic acid. So correct option is A.

questions.text: In terrestrial habitats, temperature, and rainfall conditions are influenced by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The availability of water in various forms (liquid, vapor, and ice) and its transformations play a crucial role in determining temperature and rainfall patterns in terrestrial ecosystems.
Transpiration is the loss of water vapor from plant leaves that affects local humidity and can contribute to precipitation patterns.
Thermoperiodism refers to the daily temperature fluctuations, which can be influenced by factors like sunlight and climate.
Translocation is the movement of nutrients and organic compounds within a plant and is not directly related to temperature and rainfall conditions in terrestrial habitats.

questions.explanation: In terrestrial habitats, temperature and rainfall conditions are influenced by transpiration. The rate of transpiration is directly proportional to the saturation deficit of atmosphere. Plants growing in region where transpiration is meagre do not show overheating. So transpiration prevents overheating. So option B is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following is used to determine the rate of transpiration in plants?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A hygrometer measures humidity, which can indirectly provide information about the environment but doesn't directly measure transpiration.
A potometer is a device specifically designed for measuring the rate of water uptake or loss in a plant, which is a direct indicator of transpiration rate.
An auxanometer is used to measure plant growth, not transpiration.
Tensiometers measure soil moisture tension, while barometers measure atmospheric pressure; these instruments are not used to directly determine transpiration rates.

questions.explanation: Under normal circumstances, the rate of water uptake gives a measure of the rate of transpiration. A simple potometer is a piece of capillary tubing to which a plant has been connected. The water uptake is measured by recording the time taken for a bubble in the tube to move a set distance. So option C is correct.

questions.text: Water movement between cells is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Turgor pressure is the pressure that is exerted by the fluid (usually water) inside the cell against the cell wall. It is responsible for maintaining the shape and rigidity of plant cells.

Wall pressure is the pressure exerted by the cell wall on the cell contents. It is a product of turgor pressure, which is the pressure that is exerted by the fluid inside the cell against the cell wall, and the extensibility of the cell wall.

Diffusion pressure deficit (DPD) is a concept used to describe the driving force for the movement of water vapor in and out of plant leaves through small openings called stomata. It represents the difference in water vapor pressure inside the leaf (in the intercellular spaces) and the atmospheric water vapor pressure outside the leaf.

Incipient plasmolysis is a term used to describe the initial stages of plasmolysis in plant cells. Plasmolysis is a process in which the protoplast (cell contents) of a plant cell shrinks and pulls away from the cell wall due to water loss.

questions.explanation: Water movement between cells is due to DPD. If a cell is placed in pure water it shows endosmosis and as a result, water enters into the cell. Thus, the osmotic entry of water is due to high osmotic pressure of the cell sap. The inward movement of water is, therefore due to the fact that it’s OP > TP. The net force with which water is drawn into a cell is equal to the difference of OP and TP, known as diffusion pressure deficit. DPD = OP – TP. So correct option is C.

questions.text: The most widely accepted theory for ascent of sap in trees is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Capillarity plays a role in water movement within plants, but it is not the primary mechanism for the ascent of sap.
Atmospheric pressure does not primarily drive the ascent of sap in trees.
While living cells are involved in various plant processes, they are not the main drivers of the ascent of sap.

options.text: Transpiration pull and cohesion theory of Dixon and Jolly
options.explanation: This theory proposes that water is pulled upward through the plant from the roots to the leaves by the combined forces of transpiration (water loss from leaves) and the cohesive properties of water molecules, which help maintain a continuous column of water in the plant's xylem vessels.

questions.explanation: Transpiration pull cohesion theory for ascent of sap in trees is most widely accepted. This concept was proposed by Dixon and Jolly, 1884. It is based up on three basic assumptions which are cohesion in between water molecules, continuity of water column and transpiration pull. So D is the correct option.

questions.text: In soil, the water available for root absorption is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gravitational water is the water that drains from the soil under the force of gravity and is not available for plant roots.
Capillary water is the water held in the soil against the force of gravity, and it is available for plant roots to absorb.
Hygroscopic water is tightly held by soil particles and is not readily available for plant roots.
Combined water is not a specific term related to soil water availability for plant roots.

questions.explanation: In soil, the water available for root absorption is capillary water, which is a type of soil water. Capillary water is the water that is held in the soil by capillary forces. These forces are the result of the attraction between water molecules and the soil particles. The smaller the pore size of the soil, the greater the capillary forces and the more water that can be held in the soil.When the soil is saturated with water, the excess water drains away and the soil is left with a mixture of air and water. The water that remains in the soil is held in the pore spaces between the soil particles. The water that is held in the smaller pore spaces is capillary water. The roots of plants absorb water from the soil by osmosis. The water moves from areas of high concentration (in the soil) to areas of low concentration (in the plant). The capillary water in the soil is the water that is most readily available for plant uptake. As the plant absorbs water from the soil, the water moves through the root cells and up into the stem and leaves. The water is transported through the plant in a system of specialized tissues called xylem. So correct option is B.

questions.text: The principal pathway of water translocation in angiosperms is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sieve cells are a type of cell in some phloem tissues but are not primarily responsible for water transport.
These are specialized phloem cells involved in nutrient transport but not water transport.
Xylem vessels are indeed the primary pathway for water transport in plants, but water is also transported through the phloem for various physiological processes.
Water is primarily transported through the xylem, while the phloem is responsible for the translocation of organic nutrients (sugars) in plants.

questions.explanation: The principal pathway of water translocation in angiosperms is xylem vessel system. The sap (i.e., water with dissolved minerals) is absorbed mainly by roots and is moved upward to all the parts of plants via stem. It occurs mainly through xylem. So correct option is C.

questions.text: A bottle filled with previously moistened mustard seeds and water was screw-capped tightly and kept in a corner. It blew up suddenly after about half an hour. The phenomenon involved is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Diffusion is the process by which molecules move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. This movement occurs due to the random motion of molecules, and it continues until the concentration of molecules is the same throughout the entire space. Diffusion is an important process in many biological and physical systems, such as the movement of gases in the lungs, the transport of nutrients across cell membranes, and the mixing of chemicals in a solution.

When water is absorbed during imbibition it increasesthe volume of cell enormously. Imbibition is the process by which water is absorbed by dry materials, such as seeds, causing them to swell and expand. This process is due to the ability of certain substances in the material to absorb water and swell. Imbibition is an important process in the germination of seeds and the uptake of water by plant roots. When water is absorbed during imbibition it increasesthe volume of cell enormously. When moistened mustard seeds are kept in a corner in a bottle then due to imbibition, seeds will absorb water and suddenly blew up.

Osmosis is the process by which water molecules move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration across a selectively permeable membrane. In other words, osmosis is the movement of water molecules from a solution with a lower concentration of solutes (a hypotonic solution) to a solution with a higher concentration of solutes (a hypertonic solution) through a semipermeable membrane. This process is important for many biological systems, such as the movement of water across cell membranes and the regulation of water balance in living organisms.

Diffusion Pressure Deficit (DPD) is a term used in plant physiology to describe the difference in water vapor pressure between the inside of a leaf and the surrounding air. Water vapor moves from an area of high water vapor pressure to an area of low water vapor pressure through a process called transpiration. The DPD is the difference in water vapor pressure that drives this transpiration process. The DPD is an important factor in plant water relations, as it affects the rate of water loss from the plant and can have an impact on plant growth and survival, particularly in dry environments.

questions.explanation: A bottle filled with previously moistened mustard seeds and water was screw-capped tightly and kept in a corner. It blows up suddenly after about half an hour due to the phenomenon of imbibition. The absorption of water by the solid particles of an adsorbent causing it to enormously increase in volume without forming a solution is called imbibition. So correct option is B.

questions.text: Minerals absorbed by root move to the leaf through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Minerals are absorbed by the roots of a plant through the soil and transported to the leaves through the xylem tissue. The xylem is a specialized tissue that is responsible for the transport of water and minerals from the roots to the leaves of the plant.

Minerals are transported from the roots to the leaves of a plant through the xylem, not the phloem. The phloem is responsible for the transport of organic compounds, such as sugars and amino acids, from the leaves to other parts of the plant.

Minerals absorbed by roots do not move to the leaf through sieve tubes. Sieve tubes are specialized cells that are found in the phloem tissue of vascular plants and are responsible for the transport of organic compounds, such as sugars and amino acids, from the leaves to other parts of the plant.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Minerals absorbed by roots move to the leaf through xylem. Xylem plays an important role in conduction of water. Hence, when water moves upward through xylem, minerals are also absorbed by the roots and move towards leaves through xylem only. This is known as ascent of sap. So option A is correct.

questions.text: Stomata open and close due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Circadian rhythms can influence the timing of stomatal opening and closing, but the primary control is related to turgor pressure.
While genetics can play a role in plant processes, it is not the primary mechanism for stomatal regulation.

options.text: Pressure of gases inside the leaves
options.explanation: While gas pressure changes can influence stomatal movement, the control is still primarily exerted by the turgor pressure of the guard cells.
The turgor pressure of specialized guard cells surrounding stomata controls their opening and closing in response to changes in water uptake and loss.

questions.explanation: The pressure that develops in a cell due to osmotic diffusion of water inside it, is called turgor pressure. Stomata open and close due to turgor pressure of guard cells. When turgid, they swell and bend outward. As a result, the stomatal aperture opens. When they are flaccid, the tension from the wall is released and the stomatal aperture closes. So option D is correct.

questions.text: Phenyl mercuric acetate (PMA) results in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: PMA is a toxic compound that can interfere with photosynthesis and reduce a plant's ability to produce energy from sunlight.
PMA's primary impact is on photosynthesis, and it may not directly affect transpiration.
PMA may impact respiration to some extent, but its more prominent effect is on photosynthesis.
While PMA is toxic, it may not necessarily kill plants immediately, but it can severely damage or weaken them over time due to its impact on photosynthesis and other vital processes.

questions.explanation: Phenyl mercuric acetate (PMA) results in reduced transpiration. PMA is an antitranspirant. These are some chemicals whose limited application on the leaf surface reduces or checks transpiration. A good antitranspirant increases leaf resistance but does not affect the mesophyll resistance. So option B is correct.

questions.text: Transpiration is least in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Good soil moisture means more water will be absorbed, thus increasing the rate of transpiration because the water potential increases inside the cells.
High wind velocity increases transpiration as the wind carries the evaporated water molecules quickly so more water molecules can leave the cell.

At high atmospheric humidity, transpiration is low. This is because the rate of transpiration is directly proportional to the saturation deficit. It is dependent on the gradient of vapor pressure. Thus, the higher the humidity, the lower the rate of transpiration.
High atmospheric humidity means the moisture content of the atmosphere is already greater than the cell, therefore water will tend to move inside the cell by osmosis.

questions.explanation: Transpiration is least in high atmospheric humidity. The rate of transpiration is directly proportional to the saturation deficit. In other words, the transpiration rate depends upon the gradient of vapor pressure. Hence, a t high atmospheric humidity transpiration rate is low. So correct option is D.

questions.text: Water potential is equal to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The formula for calculating water potential is Ψ = ΨS + ΨP.
The formula for calculating water potential is Ψ = ΨS + ΨP.
The formula for calculating water potential is Ψ = ΨS + ΨP.

Water potential (Ψ) is determined by taking into account two factors - osmotic (or solute) potential (ΨS) and pressure potential (ΨP). The formula for calculating water potential is Ψ = ΨS + ΨP. Osmotic potential is directly proportional to the solute concentration.

questions.explanation: Water potential is the difference in the free energy or chemical potential per unit molal volume of water in a system and that of pure water at the same temperature and pressure. Water potential is represented by Greek letter Ψ (psi) or Ψ w. Water potential is the sum total of Ψ s and Ψ p. Therefore, Ψ w = Ψs + Ψp. Hence correct option is D.

questions.text: Adult human RBCs are anucleate. Which of the following statement(s) is/are the most appropriate explanation for this feature? (1) They do not need to reproduce. (2) They are somatic cells. (3) They do not metabolize. (4) All their internal space is available for oxygen transport.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Red blood cells do not need to reproduce, so they can afford to lose their nucleus.
This is the correct option because these factors collectively explain the absence of a nucleus in mature human red blood cells.

This option is incorrect because While they are somatic cells, the presence or absence of a nucleus is not determined solely by being somatic cells. Mature RBCs have limited metabolic activity, which is related to their primary function of oxygen transport.
This option is incorrect because they are somatic cells, so the presence or absence of a nucleus is not determined solely by being somatic cells.

questions.explanation: Red blood cells of adult humans do not have cell organelles including nucleus, Golgi bodies, mitochondria, ribosomes, etc. It increases the surface area of RBCs and enables them to contain more haemoglobin (the oxygen carrying pigment. Option D is correct.

questions.text: The hepatic portal vein drains blood to the liver from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Blood from the stomach drains into other vessels, not the hepatic portal vein.
Blood from the kidneys is processed by the renal portal system, not the hepatic portal vein.
The hepatic portal vein primarily carries blood rich in nutrients and absorbed substances from the intestines to the liver, where these nutrients can be processed and regulated.
The hepatic portal vein is not directly connected to the heart; its primary role is to transport blood from the digestive organs to the liver.

questions.explanation: Blood enters the liver from two sources. From the hepatic artery, it gets oxygenated blood and from the hepatic portal vein, it receives deoxygenated blood. Blood in the hepatic artery comes from the aorta. Blood in the hepatic portal vein comes directly from the intestine containing newly absorbed nutrients, stomach, etc. Correct option is A and C.

questions.text: Name the blood cells, whose reduction in number can cause clotting disorder, leading to excessive loss of blood from the body:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Erythrocytes, commonly known as red blood cells (RBCs), are the most abundant type of blood cells in the human body. Their primary function is to transport oxygen from the lungs to all the tissues in the body and carry carbon dioxide, a waste product of cellular metabolism, back to the lungs for exhalation.
Leukocytes, also known as white blood cells (WBCs), are a diverse group of blood cells that play a crucial role in the immune system. Unlike RBCs, leukocytes have a nucleus and other organelles.
Neutrophils are a type of leukocyte and a specific subset of granulocytes. They are the most abundant type of white blood cells and are essential components of the innate immune system.
Thrombocytes are pieces of very large cells in the bone marrow called megakaryocytes. They help form blood clots to slow or stop bleeding and to help wounds heal.

questions.explanation: Thrombocytes are called blood platelets.They are minute disc­shaped cell fragments in mammalian blood. They are formed as fragments of arger cells found in red bone marrow; they have no nucleus. They play an important role in blood clotting and release thromboxane A 2 , serotonin and other chemicals, which cause a chain of events leading to the formation of a plug at the site of the damage, thus preventing further blood loss. A reduction in their number can lead to clotting factors which will lead to excessive loss of blood from the body. So correct option is D.

questions.text: Serum differs from blood in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Globulins are a group of diverse proteins that include various antibodies (immunoglobulins) involved in the immune response, as well as other proteins that transport lipids, hormones, and metal ions, among other functions.

Albumin is the most abundant protein in blood plasma and plays essential roles in maintaining osmotic pressure, transporting various substances, and regulating fluid balance between the blood and tissues.

Serum is that part of blood that is similar in composition to plasma but does not contain clotting factors of blood. It includes all proteins not used in blood clotting and all the electrolytes, antibodies, antigens, hormones, etc.
Serum contains antibodies, which are a type of globulin protein and a critical component of the immune system.

questions.explanation: Serum is the fluid that separates from blood plasma on centrifugation. Serum is essentially similar in composition to plasma but lacks fibrinogen and other substances that are used in the coagulation process.S o option C is correct.

questions.text: In mammals, which blood vessel would normally carry the largest amount of urea?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In mammals, hepatic veins normally carry the largest amount of urea. The urea cycle, also known as the ornithine cycle, is a cycle of biochemical reactions that produces urea from ammonia. Ornithine cycle takes place in the liver. So, the vein leaving the liver called the hepatic vein possesses maximum urea.

The hepatic portal vein is a blood vessel that carries nutrient-rich blood from the digestive organs (intestines, stomach, pancreas, and spleen) to the liver. It allows the liver to process and modify nutrients absorbed from the digestive system before releasing them into the general circulation. This unique vascular arrangement ensures efficient nutrient regulation and metabolic functions in the liver.
Renal vein carries clean, filtered blood out of the kidney.

The dorsal aorta is the main artery that carries oxygenated blood from the heart to the rest of the body in vertebrates. In human anatomy, it is specifically the descending part of the aorta, which runs along the back (dorsal) side of the thoracic and abdominal regions.

questions.explanation: Hepatic vein carries largest amount of urea. Urea is produced in liver. Hepatic vein transports liver’s deoxygenated blood to heart for oxygenation. So correct option is A.

questions.text: Which one of the following animals has two separate circulatory pathways?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The blood vessels of the body are functionally divided into two distinctive circuits: the pulmonary circuit and the systemic circuit. A whale is a mammal that has two separate circulatory pathways.

Sharks are cartilaginous fish, and their circulatory system is different from mammals and other bony fish. Sharks have a single-circuit circulatory system, which means that blood flows through the heart and circulates through the body in one continuous loop. Deoxygenated blood is pumped from the heart to the gills, where it is oxygenated, and then it is distributed to the rest of the body before returning to the heart.
Frogs are amphibians, and they have a mixed circulatory system that changes throughout their life cycle.
Lizards are reptiles and have a double-circuit circulatory system similar to mammals.

questions.explanation: Whale is a mammal and in mammals, two separate circulatory pathways are found — systemic circulation and pulmonary circulation. Oxygenated and deoxygenated bloods received by the left and right atria respectively pass on to the left and right ventricles. Thus, oxygenated and deoxygenated bloods are not mixed. This is referred to as double circulation. So A is the correct option.

questions.text: Doctors use a stethoscope to hear the sounds produced during each cardiac cycle. The second sound is heard when:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: AV node receives a signal from SA node
options.explanation: The SA (sinoatrial) node is known as the natural pacemaker of the heart. It initiates electrical impulses that regulate the heart's rhythm. The AV (atrioventricular) node receives the electrical signal from the SA node and acts as a gateway, delaying the signal briefly before passing it to the ventricles. However, the second sound heard through a stethoscope does not occur due to the electrical activity of the heart.

The AV valves (tricuspid and mitral valves) are located between the atria and ventricles. They open during the cardiac cycle to allow blood to flow from the atria into the ventricles. The opening of the AV valves is associated with the first heart sound (S1), not the second sound heard through a stethoscope.

options.text: Ventricular walls vibrate due to gushing in of blood from atria
options.explanation: When the atria contract and push blood into the ventricles, it does create a small vibration in the ventricular walls. However, this vibration is not responsible for the second sound heard through a stethoscope.

options.text: Semilunar valves close down after the blood flows into vessels from ventricles
options.explanation: The semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary valves) are located between the ventricles and the major blood vessels leaving the heart (aorta and pulmonary artery). They open during the cardiac cycle to allow blood to be ejected from the ventricles into the arteries. After the ventricles have contracted and ejected blood, the semilunar valves close to prevent backflow of blood into the ventricles. The closure of the semilunar valves produces the second heart sound (S2), also known as the "dub" sound, which is heard through a stethoscope.

questions.explanation: The correct option is d) Semilunar valves close down after the blood flows into vessels from ventricles. In summary, the second sound heard through a stethoscope occurs when the semilunar valves close down after the blood flows into vessels from the ventricles.

questions.text: Erythropoiesis starts in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The spleen is an organ located in the upper left abdomen. While the spleen does play a role in the immune system and the removal of old or damaged red blood cells from circulation, it is not the site where erythropoiesis (formation of red blood cells) primarily occurs.

Red bone marrow, found in certain bones of the body, is the primary site for erythropoiesis. It is responsible for producing red blood cells (erythrocytes), along with other blood cell types like white blood cells and platelets. Red bone marrow contains stem cells known as hematopoietic stem cells that give rise to various blood cell lineages, including red blood cells.

While the kidney plays an important role in the regulation of erythropoiesis, it does not directly initiate the process. The kidney produces and secretes a hormone called erythropoietin (EPO), which stimulates the production of red blood cells in the bone marrow. EPO is released in response to low oxygen levels in the blood. Therefore, the kidney indirectly influences erythropoiesis by regulating the production of erythropoietin.

The liver also plays a role in the production of blood cells, but it is not the primary site of erythropoiesis. The liver is involved in the production of certain blood components, such as clotting factors, and it can contribute to erythropoiesis in certain circumstances. However, the liver's contribution to erythropoiesis is minimal compared to the role of red bone marrow.

questions.explanation: Note: Erythropoiesis is the formation of red blood cells (erythrocytes) which occurs in the red bone arrow. Proerythroblast is the earliest precursor which gives rise successively to the early erythroblast, intermediate erythroblast and late erythroblast. Then, the nucleus is forced out and a biconcave, enucleated cell called reticulocyte is formed, which is released into blood. In blood, it develops into mature erythrocytes. Red bone marrow, found in certain bones of the body, is the primary site for erythropoiesis. It is responsible for producing red blood cells (erythrocytes), along with other blood cell types like white blood cells and platelets. Red bone marrow contains stem cells known as hematopoietic stem cells that give rise to various blood cell lineages, including red blood cells.

questions.text: Which one of the following is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Lymph is a clear fluid that circulates through the lymphatic system and plays a role in immune response. It is derived from interstitial fluid and contains various components, including lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell), but it does not contain red blood cells (RBCs) or plasma.

questions.text: Blood pressure in the mammalian aorta is maximum during:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During systole, the heart muscle contracts, and blood is pumped out of the left ventricle into the aorta, which is the largest artery in the body. This contraction generates high pressure, resulting in the maximum blood pressure in the mammalian aorta. The left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to the rest of the body, so its systolic contraction produces a surge of blood that increases the pressure in the aorta.

During diastole, the heart muscle relaxes, and the chambers of the heart fill with blood. The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body and pumps it into the right ventricle. While diastole is an important phase of the cardiac cycle, it does not generate the highest blood pressure in the aorta.

The left atrium contracts during systole to push blood into the left ventricle. However, the left atrium does not directly contribute to the blood pressure in the aorta. Its main role is to receive oxygenated blood from the lungs and transfer it to the left ventricle.

During diastole, the right ventricle relaxes and fills with deoxygenated blood from the right atrium. While this phase is important for filling the right ventricle, it does not produce the highest blood pressure in the aorta.

questions.text: The given figure shows schematic plan of blood circulation in humans with labels A to D. Identify the label and give its functions?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: C ­-Vena cava- takes blood from body parts to right auricle, pCO 2 = 45 mm Hg
options.explanation: The vena cava is a large vein in humans that carries deoxygenated blood back to the heart. There are two main types: the superior vena cava and the inferior vena cava. The superior vena cava collects deoxygenated blood from the upper body, including the head, neck, and arms, and delivers it to the right atrium of the heart. The inferior vena cava collects deoxygenated blood from the lower body, including the abdomen, pelvis, and legs, and also delivers it to the right atrium. The vena cava plays a crucial role in returning blood to the heart for oxygenation and circulation throughout the body.

options.text: D-Dorsal aorta-­ takes blood from heart to body parts, pO 2 = 95 mm Hg
options.explanation: D is the pulmonary artery. The pulmonary artery in humans is a large blood vessel that carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs. It is the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. The main function of the pulmonary artery is to transport blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs, where it undergoes oxygenation. Once oxygenated, the blood returns to the heart via the pulmonary veins and is then pumped out to the rest of the body through the aorta.

options.text: A-Pulmonary vein - takes impure blood from body parts, pO 2 = 60 mm Hg
options.explanation: The pulmonary vein is a blood vessel that carries oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the heart. It is the only vein in the body that carries oxygen-rich blood. The pulmonary vein receives blood from the pulmonary capillaries in the lungs, where oxygen is exchanged for carbon dioxide. It then transports this oxygenated blood to the left atrium of the heart, where it is subsequently pumped to the rest of the body to supply oxygen to tissues and organs. The pulmonary vein plays a crucial role in the circulation of oxygenated blood within the cardiovascular system.

options.text: B-Pulmonary artery takes blood from heart to lungs, pO2 = 90 mm Hg
options.explanation: B is the dorsal aorta. The dorsal aorta is a major blood vessel in humans that originates from the heart's left ventricle and extends down the backside of the body. It serves as the main arterial pathway, supplying oxygenated blood to various organs and tissues throughout the body. The dorsal aorta branches into smaller arteries, which further divide into capillaries to facilitate the exchange of nutrients, oxygen, and waste products between the blood and tissues. The dorsal aorta plays a crucial role in maintaining proper blood circulation and supporting the overall functioning of the human body.

options.explanation: The systole refers to the phase of the cardiac cycle when the heart muscle contracts. The P-wave on an electrocardiogram (ECG) represents the electrical depolarization and subsequent contraction of the atria, not the beginning of systole. The P-wave indicates the initiation of the electrical activity that leads to the contraction of the atria, rather than the beginning of systole itself.

The end of systole is not represented by the P-wave on an ECG. Systole encompasses both atrial contraction and ventricular contraction, and the P-wave specifically represents atrial depolarization and contraction, rather than the end of systole.

The ventricular contraction is initiated by the electrical activity that follows the P-wave, specifically the QRS complex.
