questions.text: The figure shows a human blood cell. Identify it and give its characteristics.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When observed under a microscope, basophils are a type of white blood cell that appear as large, irregularly shaped cells with dark-staining granules in their cytoplasm. These granules contain various substances, including histamine and other inflammatory mediators.The function of basophils is primarily related to their role in the immune response and inflammation. When the body encounters an allergen or an invading pathogen, basophils are activated and release their granules. Histamine, in particular, plays a significant role in allergic reactions. It causes blood vessels to dilate, increases vascular permeability, and promotes the recruitment of other immune cells to the site of inflammation.

options.text: B-­lymphocyte - Forms about 20% of blood cells involved in immune response
options.explanation: A B-lymphocyte, also known as a B-cell, is a type of white blood cell that plays a crucial role in the immune system. Under a microscope, B-lymphocytes typically appear as round or slightly irregular cells with a large nucleus surrounded by a small amount of cytoplasm. The main function of B-lymphocytes is to produce antibodies, which are proteins that help defend the body against foreign substances known as antigens. When a B-lymphocyte encounters an antigen that matches its specific receptor, it becomes activated. This activation leads to the production and release of antibodies that can bind to the antigen, marking it for destruction or neutralization by other components of the immune system. Overall, the function of B-lymphocytes is to recognize, bind to, and neutralize antigens through the production of specific antibodies, thereby contributing to the body's immune defense against infections and foreign substances.

options.text: Neutrophil - Most abundant blood cells, phagocytic
options.explanation: Neutrophils are a type of white blood cell and are easily identified under a microscope by their multi-lobed nucleus and granular cytoplasm. These characteristics give them a segmented or "polymorphonuclear" appearance. Neutrophils play a critical role in the innate immune response as the first line of defense against bacterial infections. They are highly mobile and phagocytic, meaning they can engulf and destroy invading pathogens, such as bacteria and fungi. Neutrophils are also involved in the process of inflammation and recruit other immune cells to the site of infection or tissue injury.

options.text: Monocyte - The life span of 3 days, produces antibodies
options.explanation: Under a microscope, monocytes typically appear as large, kidney-shaped cells with a single, large nucleus. They have a granular cytoplasm and irregular cell borders. Monocytes are a type of white blood cell and are part of the immune system. Monocytes play a crucial role in the immune response as phagocytes, which means they can engulf and destroy foreign particles, such as bacteria, viruses, and dead cells. They act as antigen-presenting cells, presenting antigens from the engulfed particles to other immune cells, such as T cells, to trigger an immune response. Monocytes also have the ability to migrate to tissues and differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells, further enhancing their role in immune defense and inflammation regulation.

questions.text: Which one of the following human organs is often called the “graveyard” of RBCs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The gall bladder is not known as the "graveyard" of RBCs. It stores bile produced by the liver and releases it into the small intestine to aid in digestion.
The kidney filters waste products and excess substances from the blood to form urine. It is not commonly referred to as the "graveyard" of RBCs.

The human organ that is often called the "graveyard" of red blood cells (RBCs) is the spleen. The spleen plays a role in removing old, damaged, or worn-out red blood cells from circulation. It also helps recycle components of RBCs, such as iron, for reuse in the body.

The liver is involved in various metabolic processes, including the synthesis and breakdown of molecules. While the liver can be involved in the breakdown of old blood cells, it is not commonly referred to as the "graveyard" of RBCs.

questions.text: ‘Bundle of His’ is a part of which one of the following organs in humans?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brain is not associated with the "Bundle of His." It is primarily responsible for controlling various bodily functions, thoughts, emotions, and sensory perceptions.

The "Bundle of His," also known as the atrioventricular bundle, is a specialized bundle of cardiac muscle fibers located in the heart. It plays a crucial role in the electrical conduction system of the heart, transmitting electrical impulses from the atria to the ventricles and coordinating the contraction of these chambers to facilitate effective pumping of blood.
The kidney is not associated with the "Bundle of His." It is responsible for filtering blood, regulating fluid balance, and producing urine.
The pancreas is not associated with the "Bundle of His." It plays a role in regulating blood sugar levels and producing digestive enzymes.

questions.text: Which one of the following plasma proteins is involved in the coagulation of blood?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Albumin is a plasma protein that helps maintain osmotic pressure and transports various substances in the blood, but it is not directly involved in blood coagulation.
Serum amylase is an enzyme that helps digest carbohydrates and is not directly involved in blood coagulation.
Globulins are a diverse group of plasma proteins that have various functions, including transporting antibodies and aiding in immune responses. They are not primarily involved in blood coagulation.

Fibrinogen is a plasma protein that plays a crucial role in the coagulation (clotting) of blood. During the coagulation process, fibrinogen is converted into fibrin, which forms a mesh-like structure to trap platelets and form a stable blood clot at the site of injury.

questions.explanation: Fibrinogen is a plasma protein that plays a crucial role in the coagulation (clotting) of blood. During the coagulation process, fibrinogen is converted into fibrin, which forms a mesh-like structure to trap platelets and form a stable blood clot at the site of injury.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct regarding blood pressure?

options.text: 130/90 mm Hg is considered high and requires treatment
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. While a blood pressure reading of 130/90 mm Hg is elevated, it is not necessarily considered high blood pressure that requires immediate treatment. In some cases, lifestyle modifications such as dietary changes, exercise, and stress reduction may be recommended to help manage blood pressure at this level. However, treatment decisions should be made in consultation with a healthcare professional, and a single elevated reading may not always warrant immediate intervention.

options.text: 100/55 mm Hg is considered an ideal blood pressure
options.explanation: While lower blood pressure readings are generally associated with better cardiovascular health, a blood pressure reading of 100/55 mm Hg may be considered low for some individuals. options.explanation: This statement is correct. A blood pressure reading of 190/110 mm Hg is considered very high and falls into the category of severe hypertension. Prolonged high blood pressure can pose serious health risks, including potential harm to vital organs like the brain and kidneys.

questions.text: If due to some injury, the chordae tendinae of the tricuspid valve of the human heart is partially non-­functional. What will be the immediate effect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The flow of blood into the aorta will be slowed down
options.explanation: This option is not directly related to the tricuspid valve or the chordae tendinae. The aorta is a major artery that carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle of the heart to the rest of the body. Issues with the tricuspid valve would not directly impact the flow of blood into the aorta.

The 'pacemaker' refers to the sinoatrial (SA) node, which is responsible for initiating the electrical signals that regulate the heart's rhythm. Damage to the chordae tendinae of the tricuspid valve would not directly affect the function of the SA node, which is located in the right atrium.

options.text: The blood will tend to flow back into the left atrium
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. The tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and right ventricle, so damage to its chordae tendinae would affect the flow of blood between these two chambers, not into the left atrium.

options.text: The flow of blood into the pulmonary artery will be reduced
options.explanation: Correct. The tricuspid valve is located on the right side of the heart and prevents backflow of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. If the chordae tendinae are partially non-functional, the tricuspid valve may not close properly, leading to regurgitation of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. This can result in a reduction of blood flow into the pulmonary artery, which carries blood from the right ventricle to the lungs.

This can result in a reduction of blood flow into the pulmonary artery, which carries blood from the right ventricle to the lungs.

questions.text: Which one of the following has an open circulatory system?

options.explanation: Octopuses have a closed circulatory system with a chambered heart. However, unlike humans, their blood does not flow through arteries and veins. Instead, it flows through a network of vessels and sinuses. The blood in the octopus's circulatory system is blue because it contains a copper-based pigment called hemocyanin. Hemocyanin is similar to hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying pigment in human blood, but it contains copper instead of iron.
Hirudinaria, or leeches, have a closed circulatory system.

Periplaneta, commonly known as the American cockroach, does have an open circulatory system. Open circulatory systems are found in insects and other invertebrates. In an open circulatory system, the blood flows freely in the body cavity and bathes the organs directly. There is no heart to pump the blood through the body. In Periplaneta, the blood flows through a series of tubes called hemocoel. The hemocoel is divided into two chambers: the pericardial sinus and the perivisceral sinus. The pericardial sinus surrounds the heart, and the perivisceral sinus surrounds the organs.
Of all mentioned above, only Periplaneta have open circulatory system.

questions.text: The neurogenic heart is the characteristic feature of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The neurogenic heart is a characteristic feature of most arthropods and some annelids.

The neurogenic heart is a characteristic feature of most arthropods and some annelids. In this, the heart beat is initiated by a nerve impulse coming from a nerve ganglion situated near the heart. The myogenic heart is a characteristic feature of molluscs and vertebrates. In this, heart beat is initiated by a patch of modified heart muscle itself. So, humans, rabbits and rats have myogenic heart.

questions.text: The heart sound ‘lub’ is produced when:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct option. The "lub" sound occurs when the mitral valve and tricuspid valve, both located between the atria and ventricles, close to prevent the backflow of blood from the ventricles to the atria.

options.text: The semi­lunar valves at the base of the aorta get closed
options.explanation: The closure of the semilunar valves prevents the backflow of blood from the aorta and pulmonary artery into the ventricles.
The tricuspid valve is responsible for allowing the flow of blood from the right atrium to the right ventricle during diastole.
The mitral valve opens during diastole to allow the flow of blood from the left atrium to the left ventricle.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) The semilunar valves at the base of the aorta get closed, as their closure contributes to the 'dub' sound of the heart.

questions.text: The pacemaker in the heart is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sino-atrial node, also known as the pacemaker of the heart, is responsible for initiating the electrical signals that regulate the heartbeat. It is located in the upper part of the right atrium and generates electrical impulses that spread through the atria, causing them to contract.

The atrioventricular node is located between the atria and the ventricles of the heart. It acts as a bridge between the electrical signals initiated by the sino-atrial node and the ventricles. The AV node delays the transmission of the electrical impulses, allowing the atria to fully contract before the ventricles are stimulated.

Purkinje fibres are specialized cardiac muscle fibres that rapidly transmit electrical impulses throughout the ventricles of the heart. They extend from the atrioventricular node and help coordinate the contraction of the ventricles, ensuring an efficient pumping action.

Papillary muscles are small, finger-like muscles located within the ventricles of the heart. They are attached to the chordae tendineae, which are fibrous cords that connect to the heart valves, specifically the mitral and tricuspid valves. The papillary muscles help anchor the valves and prevent them from inverting into the atria during ventricular contraction.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) atrio-ventricular node (AVN), as it is responsible for transmitting the electrical signals from the atria to the ventricles, allowing for coordinated contractions of the heart chambers.

questions.text: Blood capillaries are made of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Endothelium, Connective tissue, and Muscle Fibres
options.explanation: Blood vessels in general, such as arteries and veins, contain layers of smooth muscle fibers in addition to endothelium and connective tissue, capillaries do not have muscle fibers. Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels and are composed mainly of a single layer of endothelial cells.
Capillaries do not contain muscle fibers. Muscle fibers are typically found in larger blood vessels like arteries and veins, where they help regulate blood flow and vessel diameter.

Capillaries do have a small amount of connective tissue surrounding the endothelial layer, they do not contain muscle fibers. The connective tissue provides some structural support to the capillary walls.

Capillaries are primarily composed of a single layer of endothelial cells, forming the thin walls of the vessels. The endothelium allows for the exchange of oxygen, nutrients, and waste products between the blood and surrounding tissues. Capillaries do also have a small amount of connective tissue surrounding the endothelial layer, they do not contain muscle fibers. The connective tissue provides some structural support to the capillary walls.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) Endothelium and Connective Tissue, as capillaries are primarily composed of a single layer of endothelial cells, forming the thin walls of the vessels. The endothelium allows for the exchange of oxygen, nutrients, and waste products between the blood and surrounding tissues. Capillaries do also have a small amount of connective tissue surrounding the endothelial layer, they do not contain muscle fibers. The connective tissue provides some structural support to the capillary walls.

questions.text: Cells formed in bone marrow include:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Red blood cells, or erythrocytes, are formed in the bone marrow through a process called erythropoiesis.
Red blood cells, or erythrocytes, are formed in the bone marrow through a process called erythropoiesis. In addition to red blood cells, the bone marrow also produces white blood cells or leukocytes.
While leukocytes are produced in the bone marrow, other cells, including red blood cells and platelets, are also formed in the bone marrow.

Lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that plays a crucial role in the immune response. They are part of the adaptive immune system and are involved in recognizing and attacking specific pathogens or foreign substances. Lymphocytes are produced in various organs, including the bone marrow, thymus, and lymph nodes.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) red blood cells (RBC) and leukocytes, as these cells are both formed in the bone marrow.

questions.text: The component of blood responsible for producing antibodies is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thrombocytes, also known as platelets, are responsible for blood clotting. They help in preventing bleeding by forming clots when there is a cut or injury. Platelets are not involved in antibody production.

Monocytes are a type of white blood cell that plays a role in immune response by engulfing and digesting pathogens. They are part of the body's defense against infections, but they do not produce antibodies. Monocytes mature into macrophages, which are responsible for phagocytosis (engulfing foreign particles).

Erythrocytes, commonly known as red blood cells, are responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body and returning carbon dioxide to the lungs for exhalation. They do not produce antibodies and are primarily involved in oxygen transport.

Lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell and a component of the immune system. They are responsible for producing antibodies, which are proteins that help the body fight against infections, viruses, and other harmful invaders.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer to the question "Component of blood responsible for producing antibodies is" is D) lymphocytes.

questions.text: The wall of blood capillary is formed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hemocytes are cells found in invertebrates, particularly in the hemolymph, which is the fluid equivalent to blood. However, in the context of blood capillaries, hemocytes are not involved in forming the wall of the capillary.
Parietal cells are a type of cell found in the stomach lining that secrete hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factors. They are not directly involved in forming the walls of blood capillaries.

Endothelial cells form the inner lining of blood vessels, including the walls of blood capillaries. They are thin, flat cells that create a smooth surface for blood flow and participate in various functions such as controlling the exchange of substances between blood and tissues.

Oxyntic cells, also known as parietal cells, are the cells found in the gastric glands of the stomach. They are responsible for secreting hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factors, aiding in digestion. Oxyntic cells are not involved in forming the wall of blood capillaries.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer to the question "The wall of blood capillary is formed of" is C) Endothelial cells.

questions.text: A vein possesses a large lumen because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Tunica media and tunica externa form a single-coat
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. Veins are composed of three main layers: the tunica interna (also known as tunica intima), tunica media, and tunica externa (also known as tunica adventitia). The tunica media is a layer of smooth muscle and connective tissue that helps regulate the diameter of the vein, and the tunica externa is the outermost layer that provides support and protection to the vein. They do not form a single coat.

options.text: Tunica interna and tunica media form a single coat
options.explanation: This option is not accurate either. As mentioned earlier, veins consist of three distinct layers: the tunica interna, tunica media, and tunica externa. The tunica interna is the innermost layer composed of endothelial cells and a thin layer of connective tissue. The tunica media is the middle layer containing smooth muscle and elastic fibers. They do not form a single coat.

options.text: Tunica interna, tunica media, and tunica externa are thin
options.explanation: This option is not accurate as well. The tunica interna, tunica media, and tunica externa of veins are not considered thin. While they may be thinner compared to corresponding layers in arteries, they still have substantial thickness to fulfill their functions of maintaining structural integrity and regulating blood flow.

options.text: Tunica media is a thin coat
options.explanation: This option is the correct answer. Veins possess a larger lumen compared to arteries because the tunica media, the middle layer of the vein wall, is relatively thinner. This characteristic allows veins to have a greater capacity to accommodate blood flow and maintain lower resistance.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer to the question "A vein possesses a large lumen because" is D) tunica media is a thin coat.

Hepatic arteries carry oxygenated blood to the liver.
Systemic arteries carry oxygenated blood from the heart to various parts of the body, supplying oxygen to tissues.

questions.text: Companion cells are important in phloem tissue because they supply _ to sieve elements:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the primary role of companion cells. Water is mainly transported through the xylem, not the phloem.

This is the correct option. Companion cells supply ATP (adenosine triphosphate) to sieve elements, which are the specialized cells responsible for transporting sugars (carbohydrates) throughout the plant. ATP provides the energy needed for this active transport process.

questions.text: The main force that draws water from the soil for plants is caused by a process called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Evaporation is the process of water molecules changing from a liquid to a gas. Evaporation occurs when water molecules at the surface of a leaf absorb energy from the sun and break away from the liquid. Evaporation is a major way that plants lose water.

Transpiration pull is the force that pulls water up through the xylem vessels in a plant. Transpiration pull is caused by the evaporation of water from the leaves. As water evaporates from the leaves, it creates a suction force that pulls more water up from the roots.
Guttation is the process of water droplets forming on the tips of leaves. Guttation occurs when water moves out of the xylem vessels in the leaves and evaporates into the air.

Wilting is the loss of turgor pressure in plant cells, which causes the leaves to droop. Turgor pressure is the pressure that water exerts against the cell walls. When a plant loses water, the turgor pressure decreases, and the leaves droop.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Transpiration pull”Explanation: The main force that draws water from the soil for plant is caused by a process called Transpiration pull.

questions.text: The shrinkage of protoplast due to exosmosis is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ascent of sap is the movement of water and dissolved minerals from the roots to the leaves of a plant. It is a vital process for plants because it allows them to obtain the water and nutrients they need to survive.

Guttation is the process of water droplets forming on the tips of leaves. Guttation occurs when water moves out of the xylem vessels in the leaves and evaporates into the air. Guttation is most common in the early morning or at night when the air is cooler and more humid.

Plasmolysis is the process of a cell's protoplast shrinking away from the cell wall due to osmosis. This occurs when the concentration of solutes outside the cell is higher than the concentration of solutes inside the cell. As a result, water moves out of the cell by osmosis, causing the protoplast to shrink.
Deplasmolysis is the process of a cell's protoplast regaining its turgor pressure after being plasmolyzed.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Plasmolysis”Explanation: Plasmolysis can be defined as the shrinkage of protoplast due to exosmosis of water. When a living cell is placed in a solution having lower water potential than that of the cell, plasmolysis takes place and the cell is called plasmolysed.

questions.text: A plant requires nitrogen and sulphur for its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Nitrogen and sulfur are essential for various plant processes, but they are not directly required for the formation of cell walls. Cell walls are primarily composed of cellulose, which is made up of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen.

Starch is a carbohydrate and primarily consists of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. While nitrogen and sulfur are important for various plant metabolic processes, they are not directly needed for starch storage.

Nitrogen and sulfur are essential components of many enzymes in plants. Enzymes are proteins, and proteins contain nitrogen and sulfur in their amino acid composition. Enzymes are critical for various biochemical reactions in plants.

This is not the primary role of nitrogen and sulfur. DNA replication primarily relies on nitrogenous bases (adenine, thymine, cytosine, and guanine) and phosphates, which do not contain sulfur. Nitrogen is important for the structure of DNA bases, but sulfur's main role is in proteins and enzymes.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Enzyme”Explanation: A plant requires nitrogen and sulphur for its enzyme.

questions.text: A rye plant less than _ tall has branch roots about:

This is incorrect. A height of five meters is much taller than a typical rye plant, and the branch roots of rye do not extend to this length.

A rye plant less than half a meter tall would still have branch roots that extend to about one meter in length.

questions.explanation: nswer is “One meter”Explanation: A rye plant less than one meter tall has some 14 million branch roots of a combined length of over 600 kilometers.

questions.text: Which of the following process cause substances to move across membranes without the expenditure of cellular energy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Endocytosis is an active process in which cells engulf substances and bring them into the cell, requiring energy expenditure.

This is the correct option. Diffusion is a passive process that allows substances to move from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. It does not require cellular energy (ATP) because it occurs spontaneously as particles move down their concentration gradient.
This is incorrect. Active transport is a process that moves substances against their concentration gradient and does require cellular energy (ATP) to pump molecules or ions across the cell membrane.
This is also incorrect. Diffusion is a passive process that allows substances to move across membranes without cellular energy, so the correct answer is not "None."

questions.explanation: Answer is “Diffusion”Explanation: Diffusion cause substances to move across membranes without expenditure of cellular energy.

questions.text: The casparian strips are present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not correct. The Casparian strips are specifically located in the endodermis cells, which are found within the root cortex.
This is not correct. The pericycle is another layer in the root, but it is not where Casparian strips are found. The pericycle is associated with lateral root development.

This is the correct option. Casparian strips are found in the endodermis, which is a cell layer in the root cortex. They form a waxy, impermeable barrier that forces water and solutes to pass through the endodermal cells, ensuring selective uptake and preventing uncontrolled entry of substances into the vascular tissue.

Casparian strips are not found in the xylem, which is responsible for the transport of water and minerals within the plant. The role of Casparian strips is related to the regulation of substances entering the vascular tissue in the root.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Endodermis cells of roots”Explanation: The casparian strip separates the extracellular space in the root into two compartments: an outer compartment that is continuous with the soil water, and an inner compartment that is continuous with the inside of the conducting cells of the xylem.

questions.text: Most of mycorrhizae are present in:

Mycorrhizal associations are less common in gymnosperms compared to angiosperms, so 70% is not an accurate representation of their prevalence in gymnosperms.

This is the correct option. Mycorrhizae, which are symbiotic associations between fungi and plant roots, are most commonly found in angiosperms (flowering plants). Approximately 90% of angiosperms form mycorrhizal relationships with fungi to improve nutrient uptake, particularly phosphorus.

questions.explanation: Answer is “90% of angiosperms”Explanation: Mycorrhizal fungi get sugar, and shelter from the plant and in exchange increase the plant’s mineral nutrient uptake efficiency. Mycorrhizae are present in 90% families of flowering plants.

questions.text: The force exerted by protoplast against the cell wall is called _ potential.

The force exerted by the protoplast against the cell wall is called pressure potential.
The generator is a device that produces electricity. In the context of osmosis, a generator is a device that produces electricity by using the osmotic pressure of a solution.

questions.text: The force of attraction between water molecules is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adhesion is the force that attracts two different substances together. It is a physical force that is caused by the interaction of the molecules of the two substances. The strength of adhesion depends on the properties of the two substances and the surface area that is in contact.

The force of attraction between water molecules is cohesion. It is the attraction among water molecules that holds water together, forming a solid chain-like column within the xylem tubes. The water molecules form hydrogen bonds between them.

Tensile refers to the ability of a material to resist stretching. It is measured by the tensile strength of the material. The tensile strength is the maximum amount of tensile stress that a material can withstand before it breaks.

Imbibition is the ability of a solid to absorb a liquid. It is a physical process that is driven by the forces of adhesion and cohesion. Adhesion is the force that attracts two different substances together. Cohesion is the force that attracts molecules of the same substance together.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Cohesion”Explanation: The force of attraction between water molecules is cohesion. It is the attraction among water molecules which hold water together, forming a solid chain-like column within the xylem tubes. The water molecules form hydrogen bonds between them.

questions.text: The xylem water tension is strong enough to pull water to:

questions.text: The volume of dry seed increased by imbibitions is _ times.

questions.text: 1% of the absorbed water is used by plants in its activities during:

questions.text: In tall trees, large quantities of water are carried at the speed of:

questions.text: Total transpiration which can take place through stomata is:

questions.text: Pick up the types of transpiration which do not occur in all plants:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This type of transpiration occurs through the cuticle, which is the waxy, protective layer on the outer surface of plant leaves and stems. It varies in intensity among different plant species and is not uniform in all plants. Some plants have thicker or more impermeable cuticles, reducing cuticular transpiration.

Stomatal transpiration is the most common form of transpiration and occurs through stomata, which are present in the majority of plants. However, the density, distribution, and regulation of stomata can vary among plant species.
Lenticular transpiration occurs through lenticels, which are small openings in the bark of woody stems and roots. It is specific to woody plants and is not found in all plants.

Stem transpiration is a less common form of transpiration that occurs through the stem's surface. While it can happen in some plants, it is not a universal type of transpiration and is not found in all plant species.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Lenticular transpiration”Explanation: Lenticular transpiration is the loss of water vapours through lenticels present in the stem of some plants. All plants do not possess lenticels.

questions.text: When leaves transpire the water potential of mesophyll cells is:

options.explanation: This is not accurate. Transpiration reduces the water potential in mesophyll cells, creating a driving force for water movement out of the cells.
Transpiration affects the water potential of mesophyll cells, causing it to decrease as water is lost from the cells.

Transpiration, the loss of water vapor from plant leaves, creates a negative water potential gradient that pulls water from the mesophyll cells (inside the leaf) into the leaf's airspace and out through the stomata. This results in a decreased water potential in the mesophyll cells.

The water potential of mesophyll cells is initially increased when they take up water from the xylem. However, when transpiration occurs, the water potential decreases as water is lost, and this is the primary effect of transpiration on mesophyll cells.

questions.text: When guard cells become turgid, transpiration?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When guard cells become turgid, it means that they are filled with water and have swelled up. This causes the stomata to open. When the stomata are open, it allows for the exchange of gases, including water vapor, between the plant and its environment. As a result, transpiration, which is the process of water loss through the stomata, increases.

If guard cells become turgid but there is no effect on transpiration, it would imply that the stomata do not open or do not open fully. In such a case, the exchange of gases, including water vapor, would be limited, and transpiration would remain unchanged. However, it's important to note that in most cases, turgid guard cells lead to the opening of stomata and increased transpiration.

When guard cells become turgid and open, it results in increased transpiration. Therefore, if the guard cells become flaccid or lose turgidity, the stomata close, reducing the exchange of gases and water vapor between the plant and its environment. As a result, transpiration decreases.

If guard cells become completely flaccid or lose turgidity to the extent that stomata close tightly, it can result in the complete cessation of transpiration. Closed stomata prevent the loss of water vapor from the plant, effectively stopping transpiration. However, it's important to note that complete cessation of transpiration is not a common occurrence and usually happens under specific conditions, such as extreme drought or during the night.

questions.text: _ have the adaptations for reduced rate of transpiration.

options.explanation: Hydrophytes are plants adapted to aquatic or waterlogged environments, where water is abundant. They do not typically have adaptations for reducing transpiration since water availability is not a limiting factor in their habitats.

Xerophytes are plants that have adapted to arid or water-scarce environments. They possess various structural and physiological adaptations to reduce transpiration and conserve water. These adaptations may include features like reduced leaf surface area, thick and waxy cuticles, sunken stomata, and succulent tissues to minimize water loss.
Mesophytes are plants adapted to moderate or well-watered environments. They may have adaptations for efficient transpiration, but they are not specifically adapted for reducing it.
Halophytes are plants that grow in saline or salt-rich environments. Their adaptations are primarily related to salt tolerance rather than reducing transpiration.

questions.explanation: Xerophytes are plants that are adapted to arid or desert environments where water availability is limited. They have several adaptations to reduce transpiration and conserve water: 1.Reduced Leaf Surface: Xerophytes often have small, thick, or needle-like leaves that have a reduced surface area. This reduces the area from which water can be lost through transpiration. 2.Sunken Stomata: Some xerophytes have stomata that are located in pits or sunken cavities on the leaf surface. This sheltered placement helps to reduce water loss due to transpiration. 3.Waxy Cuticle: Xerophytes often have a thick waxy cuticle covering their leaves, which acts as a water-impermeable layer, reducing water loss through evaporation.

options.explanation: The interstitial fluid is the common environment in which cellular operations can occur. The primary function of the IF is the exchange of substances between the cells and the rest of the body. The fluid acts as a medium for the essential exchange of dissolved gases, nutrients, and electrolytes.

The extracellular fluid is the common environment in which cellular operations can occur. The primary function of the ECF is the exchange of substances between the cells and the rest of the body. The fluid acts as a medium for the essential exchange of dissolved gases, nutrients, and electrolytes.
The intracellular fluid (ICF) is the fluid within cells.

Lymph capillaries or lymphatic capillaries are tiny, thin walled vessels located in the spaces between cells (except in the central nervous system and non-vascular tissues) which serve to drain and process extracellular fluid. Upon entering the lumen of a lymphatic capillary, the collected fluid along with associated cells (notably white blood cells) is known as lymph. Lymphatic capillaries are slightly larger in diameter then blood capillaries and have closed ends. Their unique structure permits, interstitial fluid to flow into them but not out.

questions.text: Which of the following explains the binding of inorganic component in structure found in erythrocytes?

options.explanation: Carbon monoxide (CO) can bind to hemoglobin in a similar manner to oxygen, but it forms a much stronger bond with hemoglobin. The option is incorrect in saying that it binds to an "organic" portion because it binds to Fe that is inorganic.

options.text: CO2 with NH2 group of haemoglobin
options.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is mainly transported in the blood in the form of bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) rather than directly binding to hemoglobin.

The binding of the inorganic component in the structure found in erythrocytes (red blood cells) involves the binding of oxygen (O2) with the iron (Fe) component of the hemoglobin molecule. Hemoglobin is the protein present in red blood cells that is responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues. When oxygen is inhaled, it diffuses into the blood in the lungs and binds to the iron atoms (Fe) present in the heme groups of hemoglobin. Each hemoglobin molecule can bind up to four oxygen molecules, forming a stable complex called oxyhemoglobin. This oxyhemoglobin complex is then transported by the bloodstream to deliver oxygen to various tissues and organs throughout the body.

options.text: H+ with COOH group of haemoglobin
options.explanation: Hydrogen ions (H+) do not directly bind to hemoglobin. However, they can influence the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve and affect the affinity of hemoglobin for oxygen. The Bohr effect refers to the ability of hydrogen ions (H+) and carbon dioxide (CO2) to influence oxygen release from hemoglobin in response to changes in pH and CO2 concentration.

Sucrose is transported through the phloem from the source to the sink to provide energy and nutrients to various parts of the plant.

options.explanation: Diffusion is a passive process that involves the movement of substances from areas of higher concentration to areas of lower concentration. The loading of sucrose into sieve tube cells requires energy and is an active process.

Loading of sucrose from companion cells to sieve tube cells involves active transport. Companion cells actively pump sucrose into the sieve tube elements of the phloem, creating a concentration gradient that drives the movement of sucrose from source tissues to sink tissues in the plant.

Passive transport, such as simple diffusion or facilitated diffusion, does not adequately describe the mechanism of loading sucrose into sieve tube cells. Active transport is the main process involved.

Facilitated diffusion is a type of passive transport that uses carrier proteins to move substances across membranes. In the context of loading sucrose into sieve tube cells, active transport mechanisms are more relevant.

questions.text: All of the following protect the body against entrance of germs except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tears help to protect the body against the entrance of germs. They do this in a few ways: Tears wash away foreign objects and bacteria that may enter the eye. Tears contain an enzyme called lysozyme, which has antibacterial properties. Lysozyme breaks down the cell walls of bacteria, killing them. Tears also contain immunoglobulin A (IgA), which is an antibody that helps to fight off infection. In addition to protecting the eye, tears also help to keep the eye moist and lubricated. This is important because the eye does not have any blood vessels on its surface, so it relies on tears to get the oxygen and nutrients it need

Mucus membranes do help protect the body against the entrance of germs. They do this in a few ways: Mucus membranes trap germs and prevent them from entering the body. Mucus is a sticky substance that is produced by mucus membranes. It helps to trap germs and other particles that enter the body through the nose, mouth, and other openings. The mucus is then moved out of the body by the action of cilia, which are tiny hair-like structures that line the mucus membranes. Mucus membranes contain antimicrobial substances that kill germs. Mucus membranes also contain antimicrobial substances, such as lysozyme and immunoglobulin A (IgA), that help to kill germs. Lysozyme is an enzyme that breaks down the cell walls of bacteria, while IgA is an antibody that helps to bind to and neutralize bacteria. Mucus membranes produce white blood cells that fight infection. Mucus membranes also contain white blood cells, which are the body's first line of defense against infection. The white blood cells move to the site of infection and help to kill the germs.

WBCs (white blood cells) do help protect the body against the entrance of germs. They do this in a few ways: WBCs patrol the body for foreign invaders. WBCs are constantly circulating in the blood and lymph fluid, looking for foreign invaders. When they find a germ, they attach to it and engulf it. WBCs produce antibodies that attack germs. Antibodies are proteins that bind to specific antigens, which are molecules found on the surface of germs. When an antibody binds to an antigen, it triggers a cascade of events that ultimately leads to the destruction of the germ. WBCs release chemicals that kill germs. WBCs release chemicals, such as defensins and cytokines, that can kill germs directly.

Ciliated cells do help protect the body against the entrance of germs. They do this by moving mucus and trapped germs out of the body. Ciliated cells are found in the lining of the respiratory tract, including the nose, throat, and lungs. They have tiny hair-like projections called cilia that beat rhythmically to move mucus and trapped germs up and out of the airways. This process is called mucociliary clearance.

RBCs (red blood cells) do not protect the body against the entrance of germs. RBCs are responsible for carrying oxygen to the tissues and carbon dioxide away from the tissues. They do not have the ability to engulf or kill germs.

RBCs are responsible for carrying oxygen to the tissues and carbon dioxide away from the tissues. They do not have the ability to engulf or kill germs.

questions.text: The type of blood cells that have biconcave disc like shape are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, lymphocytes do not have a biconcave disc shape. Lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that are responsible for the body's immune response. They are small, round cells with a nucleus and no granules.

No, platelets are not biconcave disc-shaped. Platelets are small, disc-shaped cell fragments that are essential for blood clotting. They are not true cells, as they do not have a nucleus or any other organelles. Platelets are produced in the bone marrow from megakaryocytes, which are large cells that divide repeatedly to produce platelets.

No, monocytes do not have a biconcave disc shape. Monocytes are the largest type of white blood cell. They are round or oval-shaped cells with a granular cytoplasm. Monocytes are produced in the bone marrow and then circulate in the blood for a few days before moving to tissues, where they mature into macrophages. Macrophages are phagocytic cells that can engulf and destroy bacteria, viruses, and other foreign invaders.

Yes, red blood cells (RBCs) are the only type of blood cells that have a biconcave disc shape. This shape gives RBCs a large surface area to volume ratio, which is important for their role in carrying oxygen to the tissues.The biconcave disc shape of RBCs is also important for their ability to pass through the small capillaries in the tissues. The biconcave shape allows RBCs to flex and squeeze through the capillaries, even when they are tightly packed together. RBCs are produced in the bone marrow. They are anucleate, which means they do not have a nucleus. The absence of a nucleus allows RBCs to have a larger surface area for carrying oxygen. RBCs live for about 120 days. After that time, they are broken down in the spleen and liver. The iron from the hemoglobin in RBCs is recycled to make new RBCs.

questions.text: The cells which play very important role in developing immunity are except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Yes, monocytes play a very important role in developing immunity. Monocytes are the largest type of white blood cell, and they are produced in the bone marrow. After circulating in the blood for a few days, monocytes migrate to tissues, where they mature into macrophages. Macrophages are phagocytic cells that can engulf and destroy bacteria, viruses, and other foreign invaders.

Yes, lymphocytes are the cells which play a very important role in developing immunity. They are a type of white blood cell that are responsible for producing antibodies and mediating the adaptive immune response. There are two main types of lymphocytes: B cells and T cells.B cells produce antibodies, which are proteins that bind to specific antigens on the surface of foreign invaders. When an antibody binds to an antigen, it triggers a cascade of events that ultimately leads to the destruction of the invader.T cells are responsible for mediating the adaptive immune response. There are many different types of T cells, but they all play a role in fighting infection. Some T cells help B cells to produce antibodies, while others directly attack and kill infected cells.

Yes, neutrophils play a very important role in developing immunity. They are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the body, and they are the first to respond to a bacterial infection. Neutrophils are phagocytic cells, which means they can engulf and destroy bacteria. They do this by releasing enzymes and chemicals that kill the bacteria. Neutrophils also release cytokines, which are proteins that help to recruit other immune cells to the site of infection. Neutrophils are produced in the bone marrow and mature in the bloodstream. They have a lifespan of about 7 days. When a neutrophil encounters a bacteria, it will engulf the bacteria and kill it. The neutrophil will then die and release its contents, which will attract other neutrophils to the site of infection.

No, thrombocytes do not play a role in developing immunity. Thrombocytes, also known as platelets, are blood cells that are responsible for blood clotting. They do not have a nucleus and are small, disc-shaped cells. Thrombocytes are produced in the bone marrow and circulate in the blood. The immune system is a complex network of cells, tissues, and organs that work together to protect the body from infection. The immune system has two main types of responses: innate immunity and adaptive immunity.Innate immunity is the body's first line of defense against infection. It is made up of cells and molecules that are always present in the body and are ready to fight off infection. Innate immune cells include neutrophils, macrophages, and natural killer cells.Adaptive immunity is the body's second line of defense against infection. It is slower to respond than innate immunity, but it is more specific and can remember specific infections. Adaptive immune cells include B cells and T cells. Thrombocytes do not play a role in either innate or adaptive immunity. Their only function is to help the body clot blood.

questions.text: Antibodies are manufactured in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, antibodies are not manufactured in T lymphocytes. Antibodies are manufactured in B lymphocytes.T lymphocytes, also known as T cells, are another type of white blood cell that plays a key role in the body's immune system. T cells are responsible for helping the body to fight infection by killing infected cells and by helping B cells to produce antibodies.

No, antibodies are not manufactured in platelets. Antibodies are proteins that are produced by B cells in the bone marrow. Platelets are small, disk-shaped cells that are involved in blood clotting. They do not produce antibodies.

No, antibodies are not manufactured in red blood cells (RBCs). RBCs are the most abundant type of blood cell. They are responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the tissues. RBCs do not have a nucleus and are therefore unable to produce proteins, including antibodies.

Antibodies are manufactured in B lymphocytes. B lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that are responsible for the body's humoral immune response. The humoral immune response is the part of the immune system that fights off infection by producing antibodies. Antibodies are proteins that bind to specific antigens, which are molecules found on the surface of foreign invaders. When an antibody binds to an antigen, it triggers a cascade of events that ultimately leads to the destruction of the foreign invader. B lymphocytes are produced in the bone marrow. When a B lymphocyte encounters an antigen for the first time, it becomes activated. The activated B lymphocyte then divides and produces a clone of cells that all produce the same antibody. These cells are called plasma cells. Plasma cells secrete antibodies into the blood and lymph fluid.

questions.text: Average life span of red blood cell is about:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Yes, the average lifespan of a red blood cell (RBC) is about 120 days or four months. RBCs are produced in the bone marrow and are then released into the bloodstream. They carry oxygen from the lungs to the tissues. As RBCs age, they lose their ability to carry oxygen efficiently. After about 120 days, they are removed from the bloodstream by the spleen and liver. The iron from the hemoglobin in RBCs is recycled to make new RBCs.

questions.text: Which one has the thickest walls than the others?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, the right atrium does not have the thickest walls. The walls of the right atrium are the thinnest of all the chambers of the heart. The left atrium has thicker walls than the right atrium, and the left ventricle has the thickest walls of all.

No, the left atrium does not have the thickest walls. The left ventricle has the thickest walls of all the chambers of the heart. The right ventricle has the second thickest walls, followed by the left atrium and then the right atrium. The reason for this is that the left ventricle has to pump blood to all parts of the body, while the right ventricle only has to pump blood to the lungs. The left ventricle has to generate more force to pump blood against the higher resistance of the systemic circulation, so it has thicker walls.

No, the right ventricle does not have the thickest walls than the others. The left ventricle has the thickest walls of all the heart chambers. This is because the left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to the rest of the body, which requires a lot of force. The right ventricle, on the other hand, is responsible for pumping deoxygenated blood to the lungs, which does not require as much force. As a result, the right ventricle has thinner walls than the left ventricle.

Yes, the left ventricle has the thickest walls of the four chambers of the heart. This is because the left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood to the entire body, while the right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs. The left ventricle has to generate more force to pump blood against the higher resistance of the systemic circulation, so it needs thicker walls.The walls of the left ventricle are made up of cardiac muscle tissue, which is a type of muscle that is able to contract and relax very quickly. This allows the left ventricle to pump blood out of the heart at a high rate.

questions.text: Oxygenated blood is supplied to all body parts from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, oxygenated blood is not supplied to all body parts from the right atrium. The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body through the vena cavae. This blood then flows into the right ventricle, which pumps it to the lungs through the pulmonary artery. In the lungs, the blood exchanges carbon dioxide for oxygen. The oxygenated blood then flows back to the heart through the pulmonary veins, which enter the left atrium. The left atrium pumps the oxygenated blood to the left ventricle, which pumps it out to the body through the aorta.

No, oxygenated blood is not supplied to all body parts from the left atrium. The left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the lungs through the pulmonary veins. This blood then flows into the left ventricle, which pumps it out to the body through the aorta. The aorta branches into smaller arteries that carry blood to all parts of the body.

No, oxygenated blood is not supplied to all body parts from the right ventricle. The right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs. The left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood to the rest of the body. The right side of the heart receives deoxygenated blood from the body through the vena cavae. The right atrium receives blood from the superior vena cava, which drains blood from the head, neck, and upper body. The right ventricle receives blood from the inferior vena cava, which drains blood from the lower body.

Yes, oxygenated blood is supplied to all body parts from the left ventricle. The left ventricle is the main pumping chamber of the heart. It receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium and then pumps it through the aorta to the rest of the body. The right ventricle is the other pumping chamber of the heart. It receives deoxygenated blood from the right atrium and then pumps it through the pulmonary artery to the lungs. When the left ventricle contracts, it pushes oxygenated blood into the aorta. The aortic valve closes to prevent blood from flowing back into the left ventricle. The oxygenated blood then travels through the arteries to all parts of the body. The arteries branch into smaller and smaller vessels, called arterioles, which then branch into capillaries. The capillaries are the smallest blood vessels and they allow oxygen and nutrients to be exchanged with waste products. The blood then travels back to the heart through the veins.

questions.text: Which of these is correct about thoracic duct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It arises in the vessels of the brain
It drains the entire body above the diaphragm
It empties its contents into the subclavian vein
It carries blood into the lymphatic system

questions.explanation: The thoracic duct, also known as the left lymphatic duct, is the largest lymphatic vessel in the human body. It is responsible for collecting lymph from the lower limbs, pelvis, abdomen, left side of the chest, left arm, left side of the head, and neck, therefore option C is correct.
